evals.name: claude-md-post-refactor-default-flow
evals.prompt: yo, just refactored a chunk of ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (added like 5-6 Tier2/Tier3 entries around git ops and the gh cli) — before I start relying on it for real, can you do that multi-agent audit thing to make sure I didn't introduce contradictions or leave terms hanging? defaults are fine i think
evals.expected_output: Phase 1 symptom interview via AskUserQuestion → user selects 'post-refactor verification'. Phase 1.5 keeps full-file scope. Phase 2 confirms target=~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, target_type=claude-md (auto-detected), N=3, threshold=2, max_iterations=3, exclusions pre-loaded from references/claude-md-specifics.md and presented as multi-select. Phase 2.5 skipped (claude-md target). Phase 3 section purposes confirmed via batch AskUserQuestion. Phase 4 dispatches 3 sonnet subagents in a single tool-call message with run_in_background=true. Phase 5 produces Table A and Table B with ≥2/3 threshold. Phase 6/6.5/7/8/9/10 standard pipeline. Phase 11 anticipates auto-mode classifier (CLAUDE.md is agent config) and uses the references/claude-md-specifics.md authorization template if blocked. Phase 11.5(a) re-dispatch. Phase 12 stop check.

assertions.text: The executor identifies that this task triggers parallel-audit (not claude-md-improver, not revise-claude-md, not a single-pass review)
Phase 1 symptom interview runs first via AskUserQuestion (not skipped because the symptom seems obvious from the user's wording)
Phase 2 auto-detects target_type=claude-md from the ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md path
Phase 2 confirms N=3, threshold=2, max_iterations=3 via AskUserQuestion (the new defaults, not the old N=9/threshold=4 from cmpa)

Phase 2 pre-loads the CLAUDE.md-specific exclusion defaults — the union of references/claude-md-specifics.md (4 items: documented overrides, dual-layer designs, Tier dual management, external Claude Code concepts) and references/shared-blind-spots.md Shared exclusion defaults (subagent_type names, factored out of the specifics file)
Phase 2.5 is skipped because target_type=claude-md (only runs for skill-md targets)
Phase 3 drafts a 1-line purpose per top-level section by reading ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, then confirms the full batch via a single AskUserQuestion

Phase 4 dispatches exactly N=3 subagents in a single tool-call message with run_in_background: true, subagent_type general-purpose, model: "sonnet", using agents/auditor.md content as the prompt body (byte-identical across instances)
Phase 4 dispatch does not add 'be honest' / 'no sycophancy' instructions on top of auditor.md
Phase 5 Table B applies the ≥2/3 threshold (not ≥4/9)
Phase 6.5 dispatches a false-positive-detector subagent when ≥1 fix candidate exists
Phase 7 dispatches redundancy-checker with target_type=claude-md (so it compares against Claude Code defaults, not sibling skills)
Phase 9 dispatches one fix-safety-checker per fix candidate (or per option in multi-option mode), in parallel within one tool-call message
Phase 10 asks one AskUserQuestion per fix candidate (not batched)

Because target is ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, the executor anticipates the auto-mode classifier and is prepared to use the references/claude-md-specifics.md 'Yes, update my CLAUDE.md' authorization template if Edit is blocked
Phase 11.5(a) re-dispatch reuses Phase 3 section_purposes (does not re-collect) and updates exclusion list with anything user added during Phase 10
Phase 12 stop conditions reference the new defaults (≥(3−2+1)=≥2 of 3 say clean as practical convergence, max_iterations=3)

evals.name: skill-md-pre-shipping-with-eval-integration
evals.prompt: about to publish a new plugin in our marketplace and want one more pass on the SKILL.md before it goes out — path is plugins/parallel-audit/skills/parallel-audit/SKILL.md (relative to the marketplace root). can you do the parallel audit + also run skill-eval's static check if it's installed? defaults ok
evals.expected_output: Phase 1 symptom interview → user selects 'pre-shipping check'. The skill enforces target_type=skill-md (rejecting pre-shipping for claude-md targets). Phase 1.5 full-file scope, ensure_phase_2_5=true and ensure_phase_11_5c=true are set. Phase 2 auto-detects target_type=skill-md from the SKILL.md filename, pre-loads SKILL.md-specific exclusion defaults (subagent_type names, placeholder conventions, cross-skill informational pointers, frontmatter-delegated-to-skill-eval). Phase 2.5 runs skill-eval's static_check.py and writes static.json (because ensure_phase_2_5 is set). Phase 3 section purposes baseline. Phase 4 dispatches 3 sonnet subagents. Phase 5/6/6.5/7/8/9/10/11 standard. Phase 11 does NOT trigger the auto-mode classifier (plugin source, not installed config) so no authorization template needed. Phase 11.5(a) re-dispatch, Phase 11.5(c) re-runs static_check.py (because ensure_phase_11_5c is set). Phase 12 stop check includes the skill-eval ship-ready criterion (score==1.0 AND warnings==0).

assertions.text: Phase 1 symptom interview triggers, user selects pre-shipping; the skill validates that target_type will be skill-md (would redirect to 'post-refactor' if it were claude-md)
Phase 2 auto-detects target_type=skill-md from the SKILL.md filename in the path

Phase 2 pre-loads the SKILL.md-specific exclusion defaults — the union of references/skill-md-specifics.md (3 base items: placeholder conventions, cross-skill informational pointers, frontmatter delegated to skill-eval — plus a conditional 4th item pointing to skill-eval's static.json when Phase 2.5 ran) and references/shared-blind-spots.md Shared exclusion defaults (subagent_type names, factored out of the specifics file)

Phase 2.5 runs skill-eval's static_check.py on the target skill directory and writes the result to tmp/parallel-audit-workspace/iteration-0/static.json (because ensure_phase_2_5 is set by the pre-shipping symptom)

Phase 2 appends the conditional skill-eval exclusion item (now item #4 in references/skill-md-specifics.md after iter-4's renumber moved the subagent_type exclusion to references/shared-blind-spots.md) pointing to the static.json so Phase 4 auditors know not to re-flag the structural axes covered by skill-eval

Phase 7 dispatches redundancy-checker with target_type=skill-md (so it compares against skill-creator / skill-eval / sibling skills, not Claude Code defaults), and includes sibling_skills from glob plugins/*/skills/*/SKILL.md of the marketplace root

Phase 11 does NOT use the auto-mode classifier authorization template, because the file is in plugins/ source layout (plugin artifact, not installed config) per references/skill-md-specifics.md location table
Phase 11.5(c) re-runs skill-eval static_check.py after fixes are applied, writing to tmp/parallel-audit-workspace/iteration-N/static.json (because ensure_phase_11_5c is set)
Phase 12 stop check includes the SKILL.md-specific ship-ready criterion (Phase 11.5(c) static.json reports score == 1.0 AND warnings == 0) in addition to the generic convergence criteria

evals.name: routine-warning-abort
evals.prompt: hey, can u run that multi-agent audit on ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md? no specific issue, just doing a periodic cleanup
evals.expected_output: Phase 1 symptom interview presents 6 options; user selects 'routine'. Phase 1 emits the routine warning (cost mismatch, asymptote, lighter alternatives) and asks for explicit confirmation. The expected behavior is the executor honors the warning protocol — if the user picks 'No, I'll use a lighter tool instead', the skill aborts gracefully and suggests claude-md-improver or a single-pass Claude review. The skill does NOT silently proceed to Phase 2 just because the user invoked the skill.

assertions.text: Phase 1 symptom interview presents the 6 options including 'routine' as an explicit option
When the user selects 'routine', Phase 1 emits the warning (asymptote / cost / lighter alternatives) per references/symptom-interview-protocol.md, not silent pass-through
The warning lists at least one concrete lighter alternative (single-pass Claude review or claude-md-improver) with their relative cost
Phase 1 asks for explicit confirmation via AskUserQuestion after the warning (does not assume the user wants to proceed)
If the user picks 'No, I'll use a lighter tool', the skill aborts gracefully — does not run Phase 2 onward
If the user picks 'Yes, proceed (one-time exploratory run)', routine_override=true is set and surfaced in the final report so the user sees their override decision

evals.name: specific-rule-scope-narrowing
evals.prompt: ok so in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md I have this whole rule about using built-in Read/Grep instead of cat/grep through bash — but claude keeps falling back to `grep` via Bash for me, especially when searching across multiple dirs. can u audit just that rule + whatever sections reference it and figure out where the ambiguity is? don't audit the whole file, just that part. N=3
evals.expected_output: Phase 1 symptom interview → user selects 'specific rule ignored/misapplied'. Phase 1 follow-up AskUserQuestion asks which rule. User identifies the 'use built-in tools' rule. Phase 1.5 sets scope_hint=rule-and-neighbors, scope_target=<rule text or section heading>. Phase 4 auditors receive scope_directive telling them to read only the named rule ± 20 lines AND grep the file for terms appearing in the rule, reading any other section that references those terms. Per-instance token usage is 1/3-1/5 of full-file. Rest of workflow proceeds with the narrowed scope.

assertions.text: Phase 1 symptom interview → user selects 'specific-rule'
Phase 1 follow-up AskUserQuestion asks which rule (does not infer from the user's wording alone)
Phase 1.5 sets scope_hint=rule-and-neighbors with scope_target populated from the user's answer
Phase 1.5 confirms the chosen scope with AskUserQuestion before proceeding (one option per scope type plus 'let me specify lines')
Phase 4 dispatches subagents with a scope_directive substituted into agents/auditor.md telling them to read only the named rule ± 20 lines AND grep for cross-references — not the whole file
Per-instance token usage for Phase 4 is materially lower than full-file (target: 1/3 to 1/5 of full-file cost)
Phase 3 section_purposes still covers the full file (the intent baseline is full-file regardless of audit scope)

evals.name: should-not-trigger-on-skill-authoring
evals.prompt: thinking about making a new skill that captures the steps i use when users ask about migrating postgres data (like pg_dump → s3 → rds restore kind of stuff). walk me through writing the SKILL.md from scratch, coming up with test prompts, and benchmarking whether the skill actually helps a subagent vs just letting claude wing it
evals.expected_output: The executor does NOT trigger parallel-audit. It recognizes that the request asks for (a) authoring a new SKILL.md from scratch and (b) with-skill vs without-skill A/B benchmarking — both owned by skill-creator. The executor either invokes skill-creator directly or tells the user that skill-creator is the right tool, citing the authoring-vs-audit distinction.

assertions.text: The executor does NOT invoke parallel-audit's Phase 1 setup for this prompt

The executor recognizes that 'create a SKILL.md from scratch' + 'benchmark whether it helps' map to skill-creator's authoring + A/B benchmarking workflow, not to multi-agent prose audit of an existing file

The executor either invokes skill-creator directly or tells the user that skill-creator is the right tool, citing the authoring-vs-audit distinction (parallel-audit's 'Do NOT use' list explicitly excludes this case)

evals.name: should-not-trigger-on-source-code-review
evals.prompt: audit my auth code for bugs / security issues — main file is src/auth/session.ts but there's also some related stuff in src/auth/middleware.ts and src/lib/jwt.ts. multi-agent review would be great if possible since this is touching session handling and i want to be sure
evals.expected_output: The executor does NOT trigger parallel-audit. Even though the prompt mentions 'audit' and 'multi-agent review', parallel-audit's 'Do NOT use' list explicitly excludes auditing non-instruction files (source code, regular docs, READMEs). The executor either suggests a different code-review tool / agent or proceeds with a single-pass code review, citing that parallel-audit is for agent-instruction markdown only.

assertions.text: The executor does NOT invoke parallel-audit's Phase 1 setup

The executor recognizes that .ts source files are not the target type for parallel-audit (which is restricted to CLAUDE.md / CLAUDE.local.md / AGENTS.md / GEMINI.md / SKILL.md per the skill's description and 'Do NOT use' list)
The executor either redirects to a code-review tool / agent (e.g., pr-review-toolkit:code-reviewer) or proceeds with a single-pass code review, not parallel-audit

evals.name: should-not-trigger-on-settings-json-review
evals.prompt: can you do that convergence audit thing on my ~/.claude/settings.json? want to make sure the permissions.deny entries don't overlap weirdly with permissions.allow
evals.expected_output: The executor does NOT trigger parallel-audit even though the user uses parallel-audit's signature term 'convergence audit'. settings.json is structured JSON config, not prose instruction markdown — the 7-axis taxonomy (missing qualifier / grammar error / etc.) does not apply. The executor explains the scope mismatch and either reviews the file directly or suggests a different tool.

assertions.text: The executor does NOT invoke parallel-audit's Phase 1 setup
The executor recognizes that settings.json is JSON config, not the markdown instruction-file target type parallel-audit handles (the 7 axes assume prose)

The executor explains the scope mismatch and either reviews permissions.deny / permissions.allow directly or suggests a more appropriate tool — does not silently run parallel-audit just because the user said 'convergence audit'

evals.name: claude-md-deep-tier-n9-opt-in
evals.prompt: this CLAUDE.md is the foundation of my entire workflow — i want a really thorough audit before i ship the next batch of changes. go deep: use N=9 / threshold=4 instead of the defaults. cost is fine, i'd rather pay than miss a contradiction
evals.expected_output: Phase 1 symptom interview triggers; user picks 'post-refactor' or similar non-routine symptom. Phase 2 confirms target=~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, target_type=claude-md, with the user-overridden N=9 / threshold=4 / max_iterations=3 — the Deep tier the user explicitly opted into (not the Quick default 3/2/3). Phase 2 surfaces the Deep tier cost estimate (~440k audit dispatch + verification overhead per iter × max_iterations) before the user confirms. Phase 4 dispatches exactly 9 sonnet subagents in a single tool-call message with run_in_background=true. Phase 5 Table B uses ≥4 threshold for convergent-issue classification. Phase 12 practical-convergence row computes ≥(9-4+1)=≥6 of 9 reporting clean. Phase 12 Run parameters table reports the user's N=9 / threshold=4 (not the default 3/2).

assertions.text: Phase 2 captures the user's explicit N=9 / threshold=4 override (does not silently apply defaults 3/2 just because they are the most common)
Phase 2 cost estimate explicitly surfaces the Deep tier line (~440k Phase 4 audit dispatch + verification overhead at parent rate) before the user confirms
Phase 4 dispatches exactly N=9 subagents in a single tool-call message with run_in_background: true and model: "sonnet"
Phase 5 Table B counts an issue as convergent when ≥4 instances flag it (≈44%, not the default 67% bar at N=3/threshold=2)
Phase 12 practical-convergence row evaluates ≥(9-4+1)=≥6 of 9 reporting 'NO HIGH ISSUES' (not the default ≥2 of 3)
Phase 12 Run parameters table reports the user-overridden N=9 / threshold=4, not the default 3/2 — so the trajectory is reproducible at the deep tier
